text.title: django-imports Core Rules
text.summary: Five rules for import discipline in multi-app Django repos.

rule.statement: Cross-app imports MUST use module aliases: `from apps.users import models as users_models`, then `users_models.User`. Never `from apps.users.models import User`.

rationale.source: internal lint rule; isort first-party config

rule.rationale: Multiple apps inevitably define `User`, `Status`, `Profile`. Module aliasing prevents shadowing and makes the source app explicit at every callsite.

rule.statement: Never `from anywhere import *`. Star imports break IDE introspection and create silent shadows.
rule.rationale: Star imports hide which symbols are in scope. They also break ruff/mypy's ability to detect unused names.

rule.statement: Configure isort/ruff first-party list to include `apps`, `core`, `config`. Imports group order: stdlib, third-party, django, first-party, local.
rule.rationale: Consistent grouping aids review by separating ecosystem layers visually. Misconfigured first-party causes apps/* to land in third-party section.

rule.statement: Imports used only for type hints go under `if TYPE_CHECKING:` (or `from __future__ import annotations` with a TYPE_CHECKING block). Enforced via ruff TCH rules.
rule.rationale: Type-only imports slow startup and may create runtime circulars. TCH separates them cleanly.

rule.statement: Inside an app, model imports are direct: `from .models import User`. Cross-app: aliased (rule r1).
rule.rationale: Inside-app usage is unambiguous (only one User exists in scope). Cross-app introduces ambiguity that aliasing resolves.
